food_subgroup: Vegetable-based meat alternatives and grilled vegetable dishes

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard platter serving of Bombay Grill Vegetable Kebab is approximately 300 g, comprising multiple skewers of mixed grilled vegetables as typically served in an Indian restaurant context in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bombay-Grill Vegetable Kebab Platter — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bombay-Grill Vegetable Kebab Platter (cr_id G00010897). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on the total fat of 26.4 g/100g and the typical saturated fat fraction (~28%) for a spiced oil-marinated grilled vegetable dish with likely paneer content, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for similar preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed grilled non-starchy vegetables with protein and fat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bombay Grill Vegetable Kebab Platter. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed grilled non-starchy vegetable dishes with high fat and protein content from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g; GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 15.72 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.5, rounded to 6.1 accounting for marinade sugars. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with the food's macronutrient profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1080 mg/100g represents over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1500 mg) in a single 150 g portion, and no amount of potassium (387 mg/100g) can adequately offset this sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low GI (~35) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) and high sodium require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and estimated low GI make this dish relatively safe for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, but the high fat content (26.4 g/100g) may contribute to insulin resistance and caloric excess; limit portion to 150–200 g and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g both exceed cardiovascular-safe thresholds; cholesterol 85 mg/100g adds further concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its very high sodium content (1080 mg/100g), elevated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-salt, reduced-oil preparation and limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; nightshade vegetables likely present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in standard preparation due to very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) promoting tissue oedema, pro-inflammatory saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), and the likely presence of nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bombay Grill Vegetable Kebab Platter is an Indian-origin grilled dish served in Singapore, consisting of mixed vegetables — typically bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, and paneer or tofu — marinated in a spiced oil-based marinade and grilled on skewers. The dish is characterised by a smoky, charred exterior with tender, flavourful vegetables and a rich, aromatic spice profile featuring cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, and garlic. It is served as a platter, typically accompanied by mint chutney or raita. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (26.4 g/100g) and protein (10.7 g/100g) with low carbohydrates (5.24 g/100g), making it low-GI but calorie-dense at 302 kcal/100g. The sodium content is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is vegetarian and likely halal. It is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner in Indian restaurants across Singapore. Clinically, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile are favourable for type 2 diabetes management, but the high sodium and saturated fat content raise significant concerns for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010897, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat using a defensible fraction for this food type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed grilled non-starchy vegetables.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, though it is nutritionally unusual for a mixed vegetable dish and may reflect the specific preparation or measurement methodology. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Bombay Grill Vegetable Kebab Platter preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 7.5 g/100g based on ~28% saturated fraction of total fat (26.4 g/100g), consistent with oil-marinated grilled vegetable dishes with paneer content per USDA FoodData Central analogous entries. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed grilled non-starchy vegetables; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources.
